title: On the Structure of Cubic and Quartic Polynomials
speaker: Elad Haramaty
abstract: In our work we study the structure of polynomials of degree three and four that have high bias or high Gowers norm, over arbitrary prime fields. In particular we obtain the following results.

proof_stages.label: Finite-field non-randomness via bias
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the problem of understanding non-random cubic and quartic polynomials over finite fields and introduces large bias as the first quantitative notion of non-randomness.

proof_stages.label: Known bias-rank benchmarks and new bias theorems
proof_stages.summary: Known inverse results express biased degree-d polynomials as functions of boundedly many lower-degree polynomials, while the new cubic and quartic bias theorems give much more explicit sum-of-products decompositions with polylogarithmic or polynomial dependence on the inverse bias.

proof_stages.label: Gowers norms and inverse-conjecture landscape
proof_stages.summary: Discrete derivatives are used to define Gowers norms, the known near-extreme and inverse theorems are reviewed, and the symmetric quartic counterexample shows that the naive finite-field inverse conjecture fails in general.

proof_stages.label: High-Gowers-norm structure theorems
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states analogous structure theorems for cubic and quartic polynomials with large Gowers norm, including large-field sum-of-products decompositions and, over general fields, a quartic result saying the polynomial becomes cubic on many large subspaces.

proof_stages.label: Many low-rank derivatives from bias
proof_stages.summary: For the biased cubic proof, large bias yields many biased directional derivatives, and the quadratic biased-polynomial rank theorem converts those derivatives into many low-rank quadratic forms.

proof_stages.label: Bogolyubov passage to a derivative subspace
proof_stages.summary: A large set of low-rank derivatives is upgraded, via the Bogolyubov lemma applied to a set of directions and rank subadditivity under sums and differences, to a large subspace on which every derivative remains low-rank.

proof_stages.label: Shared linear basis for low-rank quadratics
proof_stages.summary: A subspace of low-rank quadratic functions is shown to have a small common collection of linear forms so that every member is a sum of products of one common-basis linear form with one direction-dependent linear form.

proof_stages.label: Integration back to the polynomial and extensions
proof_stages.summary: The common-basis description of derivatives is integrated by separating monomials involving the basis variables and showing the residual has only linear derivatives, after which the speaker explains how the same strategy extends to quartics and higher degrees with large-field integration and regularization obstacles.

examples.name: Highly biased Boolean function picture
examples.note: A function outputting many more ones than zeros is used to illustrate the meaning of large bias as non-randomness.

examples.name: Symmetric quartic S4 counterexample
examples.note: The symmetric degree-four polynomial is presented as having significant Gowers norm but exponentially small correlation with every cubic, forcing the move from a global inverse statement to subspace-based structure over general fields.
examples.engagement_reason: The symmetric quartic S4 is invoked as a known counterexample with stated properties, without working through its Gowers norm or correlations.

examples.name: Adding an independent variable x_{n+1}
examples.note: In response to a question about converses, the speaker notes that adding an extra independent variable to a structured-looking expression can destroy bias, showing why the stated results are one-way structure theorems rather than if-and-only-if characterizations.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the concrete operation of adding an independent variable x_{n+1} and checks its effect, namely that it destroys bias.

passes.reason: adding an independent linear variable is an ordinary construction used to refute the proposed converse characterization by killing bias
adding a fresh independent linear variable is a trivial free-coordinate construction used to show the proposed converse characterization fails
adjoining an independent linear coordinate is a trivial extension used to refute a reverse high-bias characterization
Adding the independent linear variable x_{n+1} is an ordinary construction used to refute a proposed iff characterization by killing the bias.
adding a fresh independent linear variable is an ordinary operation used to refute the converse that the displayed structural form implies large bias
adding an independent variable gives a balanced structured-looking function that refutes the proposed converse to bias characterization
Adding a fresh balanced variable is used to refute an iff characterization, since it preserves the apparent form while forcing zero bias, the minimal bias value.
adding a fresh independent linear variable is a split trivial perturbation used to refute the converse bias characterization
adding a separate linear variable gives a trivial balanced extension that defeats the proposed converse characterization
decoupled linear-coordinate extension gives exact zero bias and refutes the proposed iff characterization
Splitting off an independent linear variable is a trivial extension with exactly zero bias, used to refute the converse characterization.
